title: Chapter 10 Quiz
description: The Final Challenge: Conquer RAM and Hard Drives!

questions.question: What is the massive problem with defining an array like `int players[100];` normally?
options: The size is rigidly locked at exactly 100. If 101 players join, the program crashes. If 2 players join, 98 slots of memory are wasted completely.
It cannot be accessed by a loop.
questions.explanation: Standard arrays are strictly 'Static Allocation'. The compiler carves out an inflexible block of memory.

questions.question: What does the `malloc` function do?
options: Moves an array to another memory address.
Politely asks the Operating System for a requested number of Bytes of fresh RAM while the program is actively running.
questions.explanation: `malloc` stands for Memory Allocate. It allows apps to gracefully expand to fit their needs.

questions.question: Why must you use `sizeof(int)` when asking `malloc` for an integer array?
options: Because `malloc` only communicates with the OS in raw Bytes. It has no idea how heavy an integer actually is.
To make the memory run faster.
questions.explanation: Telling `malloc(5)` means give me 5 bytes. A single integer is usually 4 bytes! `malloc(5 * sizeof(int))` correctly calculates 20 bytes.

questions.question: What is mathematically returned by the `malloc` function if successful?
options: A Pointer pointing specifically to the very first byte of the massive new block of memory.
questions.explanation: Because it hands back a pointer, you can immediately assign it to a pointer variable like `int *arr = malloc...` and use array syntax on it.

questions.question: Memory requested by `malloc` (the Heap) is automatically deleted and cleaned up the moment your function ends.
questions.explanation: Absolutely false! Standard variables (the Stack) are auto-cleaned. `malloc` memory is permanently locked under your name until you manually free it, or until the entire application terminates.

questions.question: What is a 'Memory Leak'?
options: When variables leak into other directories.
A fatal condition where a program repeatedly `malloc`s memory but forgets to `free` it, slowly consuming all available RAM on the computer until it crashes.
When a game saves too often.
questions.explanation: A major memory leak can take down an entire operating system if the app locks up 16GB of RAM with ghost data.

questions.question: To give memory borrowed via `malloc` back to the operating system, you pass its pointer into the ______() function.
questions.explanation: `free(ptr);` revokes your ownership of the memory spanning from that specific address.

questions.question: What disaster occurs if you try to `free()` a pointer, and then print its value on the next line?
options: It prints the old value safely.
It triggers 'Undefined Behavior' (often a fierce crash called a Segfault), because you are trespassing on memory you no longer own.
questions.explanation: Never touch memory after freeing it. This is famously called a "Use-After-Free" vulnerability.

questions.question: Variables stored in RAM survive after the computer is turned off.
questions.explanation: RAM is 'volatile'. Loss of power or program termination completely wipes the memory clean. You must save to the Hard Drive.

questions.question: What object type do you use in C to interact with an open text file?
questions.explanation: `FILE *filePtr = fopen(...);` handles all the complex OS-level communication required to read drives.

questions.question: What happens if you open `"save.txt"` in Mode `"w"` (Write) but the file already contains 50 pages of text?
options: The computer asks if it is safe to overwrite.
Mode 'w' violently eradicates all old text inside the file before letting you write your first character.
questions.explanation: `w` stands for write (truncating). If you want to keep the 50 pages and just add one sentence to the bottom, use `a` (Append) mode.

questions.question: If you open a file requesting `"r"` (Read) mode, but the file doesn't exist, `fopen` returns ______.
questions.explanation: Always explicitly check `if (file == NULL)` to prevent your app from crashing when a player accidentally deletes their save file.

questions.question: What does the `fgets` function do?
options: Reads a string of text from a File stream, safely pausing before it causes a buffer overflow.
Forces a generic text string into memory.
questions.explanation: `fgets(buffer, max_chars, file)` is the exceptionally safe standard for reading file lines in C.

questions.question: The `fprintf` function works identically to standard `printf`, but requires you to provide a `FILE` pointer as the first argument.
questions.explanation: Yes. `fprintf(myFile, "Hello %s", name);` seamlessly prints formatted text directly into the open document instead of your screen.

questions.question: Why is it absolutely critical to use `fclose()` when you finish working with a file?
options: If you do not close it, the Operating System maintains a 'Lock', completely preventing the user from moving or deleting the file, and changes may not be saved.
questions.explanation: `fclose` forces the Operating System to flush all remaining text to the drive and unlocks the file for regular use.

questions.question: If you `malloc` memory, how does the memory size change if you forget to `free` it?
options: It stays perfectly rigid exactly at the size you allocated until the entire program is forcefully shut down by the OS.
questions.explanation: The OS respects your allocation. It simply waits forever until you return it.

questions.question: If `int *grid = malloc(400);` creates memory for 100 integers, can you confidently use `grid[50] = 9;` on the pointer immediately?
options: Yes, because C inherently respects pointer arrays and performs the identical pointer arithmetic under the hood perfectly.
Yes, but only in strict mode.
questions.explanation: `grid[50]` is mathematically translated directly into `*(grid + 50)`. It works identically to a stack array.

questions.question: If you open a file for Append mode (`"a"`), writing to it will place text at the absolute TOP of the file, shifting everything else down.
questions.explanation: Append mode strictly locks the cursor to the absolute BOTTOM (end) of the file, adding completely new text there.

questions.question: What is the number one golden rule of Dynamic Memory Management?
options: If you don't know the exact size, ask for 1 Gigabyte.
For every single `malloc` you call, you must guarantee there is exactly one `free` call to clean it up before the code completes.
questions.explanation: If `mallocs` > `frees`, you have a Memory Leak. If `frees` > `mallocs`, you have a Double-Free Crash.

questions.question: You have survived the C course! What are the primary strengths of the C programming language?
options: It runs through a slow Virtual Machine interpretation engine with complex object hierarchies.
It lacks memory management, making it safe.
It provides raw, brutally unabstracted access to the absolute hardware RAM, allowing for lighting-fast performance explicitly tuned to the metal.
It is designed solely for web apps.
questions.explanation: C is the language of operating systems, game engines, and embedded microcontrollers. It sacrifices safety for raw, unadulterated speed.
